title: Inference Optimization — KV Cache, FlashAttention & Quantization
description: LLM inference is the dominant cost at scale — optimizing it is a Staff Engineer's top priority. Understand KV cache memory, FlashAttention, speculative decoding, and quantization to reduce latency and cost by 5-20×.

sections.title: The Inference Problem: Memory-Bound Generation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM inference is memory-bound, not compute-bound. Each generated token requires reading the entire model weights from HBM to SRAM. The bottleneck is memory bandwidth, not FLOPs.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: A100: 2TB/s HBM bandwidth, 312 TFLOPS. For a 7B model (14GB in FP16): minimum latency = 14GB / 2TB/s = 7ms per token. Real latency is ~20ms/token due to attention overhead. For 70B (140GB): minimum = 70ms, real = ~100ms/token.

track_changes Practical Rule: If you need <50ms per token, a 7B model is the maximum size for a single A100 without quantization.
sections.codeBlock:
# Memory bandwidth bound calculation
def min_token_latency(model_params_b, hbm_bandwidth_gbps=2000, bytes_per_param=2):
    model_gb = model_params_b * bytes_per_param
    return model_gb / hbm_bandwidth_gbps * 1000  # ms

for size, name in [(7, '7B'), (13, '13B'), (70, '70B'), (405, '405B')]:
    print(f"{name}: min_latency={min_token_latency(size):.1f}ms/token")
# Real latency is 2-3× higher due to attention + overhead

sections.title: KV Cache: Pre-Fill vs Decode Phase
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: During pre-fill (processing the input prompt), the KV cache is populated for all input tokens in parallel. During decode (generating each new token), the KV cache is read to compute attention for the new token against all previous tokens.

bar_chart Memory formula: 2 × n_layers × n_kv_heads × d_head × seq_len × 2_bytes. For 70B model, GQA(8), d_head=128, 128K context: 2×80×8×128×131072×2 = 42.9 GB. The KV cache alone exceeds the VRAM of a single A100 80GB.

search Interview Angle: 'Your 70B model runs out of memory at 50K tokens. Diagnose and fix.' Answer: KV cache consumes 2×80×8×128×50K×2 = 16.4GB. Together with model weights (140GB), this exceeds 80GB. Solutions: GQA reduces KV heads, INT8 quantization reduces weights to 70GB, FlashAttention reduces activation memory.
sections.codeBlock:
def kv_cache_gb(n_layers, n_kv_heads, d_head, seq_len, bytes_per=2):
    kv_per_layer = 2 * n_kv_heads * d_head * seq_len * bytes_per
    return n_layers * kv_per_layer / 1e9

# Compare across scales
configs = [
    (32, 8, 128, 8192, '7B, 8K'),
    (80, 8, 128, 32768, '70B, 32K'),
    (80, 8, 128, 131072, '70B, 128K'),
    (126, 16, 128, 131072, '405B, 128K'),
]
for l, kv, d, ctx, name in configs:
    print(f'{name}: KV cache = {kv_cache_gb(l, kv, d, ctx):.1f} GB')
# At 128K context, KV cache dominates memory budget

sections.title: FlashAttention: Fused Attention Kernel
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Standard attention computes S=QK^T (N² matrix) and stores it in HBM. FlashAttention tiles the computation — each tile of Q, K, V is loaded into fast SRAM, the partial result is computed, and the final output accumulated without ever writing the full N×N matrix.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: FlashAttention-2 achieves ~120 TFLOPS on A100 for 4K context (vs ~35 TFLOPS standard). At 128K context: 10,000× less memory (32GB → 3MB). FlashAttention-3 adds FP8 support on H100/Blackwell.

track_changes Practical Rule: Always use FlashAttention-2 for training and inference at any context length > 2K. Never use standard attention.
sections.codeBlock:
def attn_memory(N, D):
    std = N * N * 2  # fp16 scores matrix, bytes
    block = 128
    flash = block * block * 2  # tiled in SRAM
    return std/1e9, flash/1e9

for N in [2048, 8192, 32768, 131072]:
    s, f = attn_memory(N, 128)
    print(f'N={N:7d}: std={s:.2f}GB, flash={f:.4f}GB')
# 128K: 32GB vs 0.00003GB → 1,000,000× reduction
# ponytail: real FlashAttention also uses online rescaling

sections.title: PagedAttention & vLLM
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: KV cache memory is fragmented — sequences have different lengths, start/end at different times. PagedAttention applies OS-style virtual memory: contiguous logical pages map to non-contiguous physical blocks. Copy-on-write for shared prefixes (same system prompt across requests).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: vLLM reduces KV cache fragmentation by 95%, improving throughput 2-4× over naive caching. Each page = 16 tokens (16 × d_head × 2 bytes). Page table overhead is ~0.1% of KV cache size.

search Interview Angle: 'How would you serve 100 different system prompts with maximum KV reuse?' Use vLLM's automatic prefix caching — if two requests share a prefix, the KV cache is shared via copy-on-write. For static system prompts, pre-compute their KV cache and share across all requests.
sections.codeBlock:
# PagedAttention conceptual memory layout
class PagedKV:
    def __init__(self, page_size=16, num_layers=32, num_heads=8, head_dim=128):
        self.page_size = page_size  # tokens per page
        self.page_bytes = 2 * page_size * num_heads * head_dim * 2  # K+V in FP16
        self.blocks = {}  # logical page -> physical block
        self.page_table = {}  # sequence_id -> [page_ids]
    
    def alloc_page(self):
        phys_block = len(self.blocks)
        self.blocks[phys_block] = torch.zeros(self.page_bytes // 2, dtype=torch.float16)
        return phys_block
    
    def share_prefix(self, seq_a, seq_b):
        """Copy-on-write: share pages until a write occurs."""
        shared_pages = min(len(self.page_table[seq_a]), len(self.page_table[seq_b]))
        self.page_table[seq_b] = self.page_table[seq_a][:shared_pages].copy()
        # Actual pages are shared in memory via reference counting

sections.title: Speculative Decoding
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Use a small draft model (e.g., 100M params) to generate K tokens cheaply, then verify all K with the large target model in a single forward pass. If the draft is accepted (matches target distribution), you get K tokens for the cost of 1 target forward pass + K draft passes.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Draft 7B → target 70B, K=5. When acceptance rate = 80%, speedup = 2.5×. Acceptance rate varies by task: simple QA (code completion) = 85-90%, creative writing = 60-70%. Draft model should be 1/10th the size of the target.

track_changes Practical Setup: Use a quantized (INT4) version of the same model family as the draft (e.g., Llama 3 8B INT4 drafts for Llama 3 70B FP16). The draft and target share tokenizer and vocabulary.
sections.codeBlock:
def speculative_decode(target, draft, prefix, K=5, temperature=0.0):
    """
    Generate K tokens with draft, verify with target.
    """
    # Draft generates K candidates
    draft_tokens = draft.generate(prefix, max_new_tokens=K, temperature=temperature)
    
    # Target verifies all at once (single forward pass)
    target_logits = target.forward(prefix + draft_tokens)
    
    # Rejection sampling: accept token if draft_prob <= target_prob
    accepted = []
    for i, token in enumerate(draft_tokens):
        target_p = F.softmax(target_logits[len(prefix) + i], dim=-1)
        draft_p = draft.forward(prefix + draft_tokens[:i])[0][-1]
        
        if temperature == 0.0:
            # Greedy: accept if target argmax matches draft
            if target_p.argmax() == token:
                accepted.append(token)
            else:
                break
        else:
            target_p = target_p[token].item()
            draft_p = draft_p[token].item()
            if target_p >= draft_p or random.random() < target_p / draft_p:
                accepted.append(token)
            else:
                break
    
    return accepted  # average 3-4 tokens with K=5, 80% acceptance

sections.title: Quantization: INT8, INT4, GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Quantization reduces model weight precision to fit in less VRAM. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) rounds weights to lower precision without retraining. Calibration-based methods (GPTQ, AWQ) minimize quantization error by observing activation distributions on calibration data.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: 70B model memory: FP16=140GB, INT8=70GB, INT4=35GB. GPTQ: layer-wise Hessian-based rounding, gold standard for INT4. AWQ: protects the 1% of weights at high-activation channels — matches FP16 quality at INT4 on most tasks. GGUF (llama.cpp): CPU-friendly format with mixed quantization per layer.

track_changes Practical Workflow: For deployment: try INT8 first (safest, <1% quality loss). If you need INT4: use AWQ (better quality than GPTQ at same bit-width). For CPU inference: use GGUF with Q4_K_M (good quality/speed tradeoff).
sections.codeBlock:
# Memory comparison for quantization
params = 70e9
for bits, name in [(16, 'FP16'), (8, 'INT8'), (4, 'INT4')]:
    gb = params * bits / 8 / 1e9
    print(f'{name}: {gb:.0f} GB')

# Naive INT8 quantization
def quantize_int8(w):
    scale = w.abs().max() / 127.0
    q = torch.round(w / scale).clamp(-128, 127).to(torch.int8)
    return q, scale

def dequantize(q, scale):
    return q.float() * scale

sections.title: Prefix Caching & Prompt Caching
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: If two requests share a prefix (same system prompt, same few-shot examples), the KV cache for that prefix can be reused. This eliminates the pre-fill cost for the shared portion.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: For chat applications with 500-token system prompts: cache hit rate >80%, saving 30-60% of decode cost. OpenAI's automatic prompt caching (2024) gives 50% discount on cached tokens. vLLM's prefix caching with hash-based matching achieves ~95% hit rate for shared system prompts.

track_changes Practical Setup: Use vLLM's automatic prefix caching (enabled by default). For maximum reuse: keep system prompts identical across requests, prepend shared few-shot examples at the beginning. Cache LRU eviction with 1M+ token capacity.

sections.title: Inference Cost Modeling
sections.description:
bar_chart Cost per token: A100 at $2/hr, 7B model at 100 tok/s/GPU = $0.56/M tokens. 70B at 15 tok/s/GPU = $3.70/M tokens. Quantized (INT4) 70B at 25 tok/s/GPU = $2.22/M tokens.

track_changes Capacity Planning: 10M queries/day, avg 1000 input + 200 output tokens = 12B tokens/day. 7B: 12B / (100 tok/s × 86400 s) = 1.4 GPUs. With 3× redundancy = 5 GPUs. Cost: $2.5K/month for 5× A100s. 70B: 12B / (15 × 86400) = 9.3 GPUs + 3× = 28 GPUs. Cost: $14K/month.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'Design a serving architecture for 100M queries/day with <200ms latency.' Use 7B AWQ INT4 (fastest per token) with speculative decoding (draft = 100M model). Deploy on A10G (cheaper than A100 for inference). Continuous batching with vLLM. Load balance across 40 GPUs with 30% headroom. Total monthly cost: ~$15K.
sections.codeBlock:
def inference_cost(tokens_per_month, tok_per_sec_per_gpu, gpu_cost_per_hr=2.0):
    seconds_needed = tokens_per_month / tok_per_sec_per_gpu
    gpu_hours = seconds_needed / 3600
    return gpu_hours * gpu_cost_per_hr, gpu_hours / (720 * 0.8)  # cost, GPUs at 80% util

queries = 10e6  # per day
tokens_per_query = 1200  # 1000 input + 200 output
tokens_monthly = queries * 30 * tokens_per_query

for name, tok_s in [('7B FP16', 100), ('70B FP16', 15), ('70B INT4', 25)]:
    cost, gpus = inference_cost(tokens_monthly, tok_s)
    print(f'{name}: ${cost/1e3:.1f}K/mo, {gpus:.0f} GPUs')

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• FlashAttention — Fast and memory-efficient exact attention
  • PagedAttention — vLLM's OS-style KV cache management
  • Speculative Decoding — Fast inference via draft-verify
  • AWQ — Activation-aware weight quantization
  • GPTQ — Post-training quantization for LLMs

details: Staff Engineer Summary: Inference optimization has 5 major levers: 1) KV cache (GQA, MLA, page table) — reduces memory 4-10×. 2) FlashAttention — makes attention compute-bound instead of memory-bound. 3) Speculative decoding — 2-3× throughput improvement when acceptance rate is high. 4) Quantization (AWQ INT4) — 4× memory reduction with negligible quality loss. 5) Continuous batching — 5-20× throughput over static batching. The combined effect: a 70B model can be served at 70B-quality with 7B-level cost via INT4 + speculative decoding + GQA.
